kata.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
kata.workload: at-most-two-transactions DP (relax buy1/sell1/buy2/sell2 across one pass, answer sell2) over N=2,000,000 LCG prices in [1,4096], K=10 SERIAL outer calls (each perturbs the opening price by the previous result — a single-threaded dependency chain matching the mirrors), sum-of-results sink

env.note: Measured on a shared x86-64 Linux cloud container (karac from kara main), NOT the corpus's Apple M5 Pro. Absolute times/sizes/RSS NOT comparable across hosts; only within-file cross-language ratios are the signal. Seq lane (fastest first): c 42ms ~ rust 43ms < go 47ms ~ rust_ovf 48ms ~ kara 49ms. RESULT: kara is at EQUAL-SAFETY PARITY with rust_ovf (both ~48-49ms, ~1.15x behind wrapping rust -O / C). The ~1.15x vs rust -O is the OVERFLOW-CHECK tax (the four-state DP has 4 overflow-checked ops per element), not a codegen gap: kara's inner loop is 4 branchless cmovg relaxations (buy1/sell1/buy2/sell2) + 4 jo overflow checks, with the forward index prices[i] bounds-check-elided (1 panic site in the whole binary) — the same instruction budget rust_ovf carries. SINGLE-THREADED: the K=10 outer loop is intentionally a SERIAL dependency chain (each rep perturbs data[0] by the previous result); a naive `sum += max_profit(data)` reduce would auto-parallelize under kara's default (raising CPU to ~200% and adding scheduling variance), which would be an unfair comparison against the single-threaded mirrors — the serial loop keeps kara sequential like #121/#122. All mirrors agree on the sink; correctness verified across interp/JIT/AOT/auto-par and vs a four-state==general-k-DP==brute-force ground truth. Python (timed separately) omitted. Re-run bench.sh on the M5 for a canonical results.json.
